metadata.title: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: So Rayleigh-Jeans hands every mode k-B times T and the mode count explodes at short wavelengths, while Planck's rule that a mode needs a whole h-nu quantum starves the high frequencies. I want to compute the rescue. Please: (1) compare k-B T at 3000 Kelvin against h-nu for a 600-nanometer mode and show the eight-fold gap, (2) write the Planck factor that suppresses that mode, and (3) derive how the peak wavelength shifts if I heat the box to 6000 Kelvin.

props.command: So Rayleigh-Jeans hands every mode k-B times T and the mode count explodes at short wavelengths, while Planck's rule that a mode needs a whole h-nu quantum starves the high frequencies. I want to compute the rescue. Please: (1) compare k-B T at 3000 Kelvin against h-nu for a 600-nanometer mode and show the eight-fold gap, (2) write the Planck factor that suppresses that mode, and (3) derive how the peak wavelength shifts if I heat the box to 6000 Kelvin.
props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow

beats.narration_text: Classical physics made a prediction: every warm object should glow with infinite energy at short wavelengths. Every oven, every candle, every star — infinitely bright. The universe is still here. Something was deeply wrong.

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: how did Max Planck fix the ultraviolet catastrophe?

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: blackbody radiator at 3000K — classical wave modes filling a cavity, energy density increasing without bound at short wavelengths"

beats.narration_text: Heat a metal box to 3000 Kelvin. The walls radiate and re-absorb light continuously. Classical physics predicts exactly how much energy each frequency mode should carry — and the answer blows up.

The Rayleigh-Jeans law: each wave mode gets k-B times T of energy on average, no matter how short its wavelength. More modes fit at shorter wavelengths — infinitely many. Multiply them out and you get infinite total energy. This is wrong.

card.copy: Classical law: every wave mode gets the same energy. Shorter wavelengths: more modes. Total energy: infinite. But real ovens don't explode. Why?

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: the ultraviolet catastrophe — Rayleigh-Jeans law plotted, energy climbing steeply at short wavelengths with no upper bound"

beats.narration_text: The classical curve climbs steeply at short wavelengths — more and more energy poured into higher and higher frequencies. This is the ultraviolet catastrophe.

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: Planck's quantum fix — discrete energy packets of size hν gating each mode, high-frequency modes starved because kT is less than hν"

beats.narration_text: Planck's fix: light energy comes in discrete packets — quanta — of size h times frequency. A high-frequency mode can only be excited if it gets one whole quantum. If k-B T is less than h-nu, the mode stays empty.

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: high-frequency oscillator modes staying empty — thermal energy kT = 0.26 eV is far below one visible-light quantum hν = 2.1 eV, modes contribute nothing"

beats.narration_text: High-frequency modes are starved: thermal energy is not enough to buy even one quantum. They contribute almost nothing. The energy curve bends back down at short wavelengths.

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: Planck distribution vs Rayleigh-Jeans on the same axes — Planck peaks and falls, Rayleigh-Jeans diverges, both normalized to Planck peak"

beats.narration_text: The Planck curve peaks at a wavelength set by the temperature — hotter boxes peak at shorter wavelengths — then falls back to zero. This is the blackbody spectrum, and it matches the data exactly.

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: discrete energy billing — an oscillator receiving only whole quanta hν, bar chart of allowed energies contrasted with classical continuous delivery"

beats.narration_text: The key insight: energy is not continuous. You cannot give a high-frequency oscillator a little energy — it must receive a full quantum h-nu or nothing. Nature bills in whole packets, not fractions.

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: blackbody example at 3000K — thermal energy kT = 0.26 eV vs visible photon hν = 2.1 eV at 600 nm, show the 8x ratio as a visual gap"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: for a box at 3000 Kelvin, k-B T is about 0.26 eV. A visible-light mode at 600 nanometers has a quantum h-nu of about 2.1 eV — eight times larger than the thermal budget. Most visible modes stay nearly empty.
Classical physics gives each mode equal energy and gets infinity. Quantum mechanics gates each mode with a minimum — and gets the right answer.

card.copy: Classical: every mode gets equal energy. Quantum: each mode needs one full packet. High frequencies stay empty. The curve bends back down.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Three temperatures — a forge, the Sun, a hot star. Ask Claude to plot all three Planck curves alongside the classical divergence. See the catastrophe and the fix together.

props.segment: Why a Warm Box Doesn't Glow With Infinite Energy
props.command: plot the Planck blackbody spectrum for three temperatures: 3000K, 5778K (the Sun), and 10000K — and on the same axes show the Rayleigh-Jeans classical prediction diverging

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Classical physics gives each mode equal energy and gets infinity. Quantum mechanics gates each mode with a minimum — and gets the right answer. Your turn. Three temperatures — a forge, the Sun, a hot star. Ask Claude to plot all three Planck curves alongside the classical divergence. See the catastrophe and the fix together.
